Threat: Ecosystem Value Index may not fully represent mission-oriented value
Potential Impact: The study may overstate MOEE performance if EVI weights omit important stakeholder values.
Mitigation Strategy: Define EVI components explicitly, justify all weights, conduct sensitivity analysis, and report disaggregated metrics.
Residual Limitation: No single index can fully capture all stakeholder value trade-offs.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Weight-sensitivity plots; comparison of aggregate EVI with stakeholder-specific utility scores

Threat: Mission Achievement may be simplified into a weighted metric
Potential Impact: Complex institutional missions may be reduced to measurable proxies only.
Mitigation Strategy: Separate mission indicators from operational metrics and explain which mission dimensions are not yet captured.
Residual Limitation: Qualitative mission meanings may remain partially unmodeled.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Traceability matrix linking mission statements to measurable variables

Threat: Stakeholder utility functions may reflect researcher assumptions rather than actual preferences
Potential Impact: Recommendations may optimize a utility model that differs from real stakeholder priorities.
Mitigation Strategy: Treat utilities as explicit modeling assumptions, calibrate with surveys or interviews when available, and test multiple utility weight sets.
Residual Limitation: Preferences may change over time and under stress.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Stakeholder validation session; sensitivity analysis across utility weights

Threat: Observed performance gains may be caused by parameter choices rather than MOEE architecture
Potential Impact: MOEE advantage may be inflated if default parameters favor coordination.
Affected MOEE Component: Simulation parameters; scenario engine; MSRS policies
Mitigation Strategy: Use identical base parameters across models, disclose all parameters, run multiple parameter sets, and perform ablation tests.
Residual Limitation: Some parameters may remain uncertain before field calibration.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Parameter-sensitivity table; ablation of PUDAL, MSRS, and learning components

Threat: Comparison between Baseline, Online Platform, and MOEE may not be equally calibrated
Potential Impact: Differences may reflect model implementation imbalance rather than conceptual superiority.
Mitigation Strategy: Define the same demand, capacity, cost, and scenario conditions for all models; document what capabilities each model is allowed to use.
Residual Limitation: Real-world baseline and platform implementations vary substantially.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Common experimental-control matrix; capability comparison table

Threat: Recommendation acceptance behavior may be overly idealized
Potential Impact: MOEE may appear stronger if stakeholders accept recommendations more often than realistic.
Affected MOEE Component: MSRS; PUDAL Action stage; stakeholder agents
Mitigation Strategy: Model acceptance probability as a variable, test low/medium/high trust settings, and report acceptance-rate sensitivity.
Residual Limitation: Actual human adoption depends on culture, incentives, trust, and organizational politics.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Scenario runs with acceptance rates from 0% to 100%

Threat: PUDAL Learning may not be fully implemented in early Mini-Lab versions
Potential Impact: The simulation may evaluate closed-loop coordination but not genuine adaptive learning.
Mitigation Strategy: Clearly distinguish logging, parameter update, and adaptive learning; label future-learning results as placeholders if not implemented.
Residual Limitation: Early results may understate or overstate the role of learning depending on assumptions.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Code trace showing whether parameters update across time steps

Threat: Campus food service case may not generalize to other mission-oriented ecosystems
Potential Impact: Findings may be case-specific and not automatically transferable to GRACE, water, loans, or healthcare.
Mitigation Strategy: Identify which elements are domain-independent and which are domain-specific; include a cross-domain generalization table.
Residual Limitation: Each domain requires new stakeholders, missions, resources, and constraints.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Mapping of PSKVE, RMOV, MIG, PUDAL, and MSRS across domains

Threat: Scenario library may not represent the full range of real disruptions
Potential Impact: Robustness claims may not hold under untested combinations of shocks.
Mitigation Strategy: Use diverse scenarios, include compound disruptions, and avoid claiming universal robustness.
Residual Limitation: Rare black-swan events and institutional shocks may remain outside the library.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Scenario coverage matrix; stress-test expansion log

Threat: Stakeholder behavior in simulation may differ from field behavior
Potential Impact: Agent-level responses may not capture habit, resistance, informal negotiation, or strategic gaming.
Affected MOEE Component: Stakeholder agents; utility functions; acceptance model
Mitigation Strategy: Use stakeholder interviews or field pilots for calibration and present agent behavior as a transparent assumption.
Residual Limitation: Human behavior remains partly unpredictable and context-sensitive.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Behavioral calibration data; pilot comparison between simulated and observed behavior

Threat: Insufficient simulation repetitions may lead to unstable statistical conclusions
Potential Impact: Reported differences may be artifacts of random seeds or stochastic noise.
Mitigation Strategy: Run repeated simulations with shared seed sets, report confidence intervals, and conduct appropriate statistical tests.
Residual Limitation: Very high-dimensional experiments may still have limited statistical power.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Confidence intervals; convergence of mean EVI over repetitions

Threat: Multiple comparisons across models, policies, metrics, and scenarios may inflate significance
Potential Impact: Some reported significant differences may be false positives.
Mitigation Strategy: Use correction procedures or emphasize effect sizes and robustness rather than p-values alone.
Residual Limitation: Exploratory simulation studies may generate many correlated comparisons.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Adjusted p-values; effect-size table; robustness ranking

Threat: Aggregate ranking may hide stakeholder-specific losses
Potential Impact: A high-ranked MSRS policy may improve total EVI while harming a minority stakeholder group.
Mitigation Strategy: Report disaggregated stakeholder utility, fairness metrics, and minimum-threshold constraints.
Residual Limitation: Value trade-offs cannot always be eliminated.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Stakeholder utility distribution; Gini or fairness index

Threat: Default parameters may not be calibrated with real campus data
Potential Impact: Numerical results may be illustrative rather than empirically valid.
Mitigation Strategy: Label default values as placeholders, collect field data, and separate conceptual proof-of-concept from empirical validation.
Residual Limitation: Some data may be unavailable, sensitive, or costly to collect.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Parameter provenance table; calibration status column

Threat: Data from vendors, students, suppliers, or university systems may be incomplete or biased
Potential Impact: Perception and Understanding stages may produce distorted ecosystem state estimates.
Mitigation Strategy: Use validation checks, missing-data handling, triangulation across sources, and uncertainty reporting.
Residual Limitation: Data quality may vary by stakeholder and time period.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Missing-data report; anomaly log; source comparison

Threat: PSKVE exchanges may be simplified as direct flows
Potential Impact: Complex institutional, relational, or symbolic exchanges may be omitted.
Mitigation Strategy: Explicitly define which exchange types are modeled and add qualitative explanation for omitted exchanges.
Residual Limitation: Some knowledge, trust, and legitimacy flows are difficult to quantify.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Exchange matrix completeness review; stakeholder validation

Threat: RMOV account updates may not capture real resource depletion, recovery, or value conversion
Potential Impact: Stakeholder sustainability may be misestimated over long simulation horizons.
Mitigation Strategy: State update equations clearly, calibrate with domain data, and test alternative account-update rules.
Residual Limitation: Some resources and values are intangible and hard to monetize or normalize.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Account-balance trajectory; equation sensitivity analysis

Threat: Mission dependency matrix may omit latent or indirect dependencies
Potential Impact: Bottleneck and synchronization analysis may miss important systemic risks.
Affected MOEE Component: Mission Dependency Matrix; MIG; PUDAL Understanding
Mitigation Strategy: Use iterative dependency elicitation, scenario stress tests, and stakeholder review.
Residual Limitation: Hidden dependencies may only appear during real disruptions.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Dependency review workshop; scenario failure analysis

Threat: Software bugs or inconsistent module integration may affect simulation results
Potential Impact: Performance differences may reflect coding errors rather than model behavior.
Affected MOEE Component: Mini-Lab software modules; simulation runner; metrics engine
Mitigation Strategy: Use unit tests, smoke tests, code review, reproducible seeds, and independent result checks.
Residual Limitation: Research prototypes may not reach production-grade reliability.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Test coverage report; reproducibility check; independent metric recomputation

Threat: Dashboard visualizations may encourage overinterpretation of placeholder results
Potential Impact: Users may treat illustrative outputs as validated empirical findings.
Affected MOEE Component: Digital Twin Mini-Lab dashboard; result export
Mitigation Strategy: Clearly label placeholder, simulated, calibrated, and field-validated outputs.
Residual Limitation: Visual interfaces can make uncertain outputs appear authoritative.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: UI labels; result metadata; calibration-status field

Threat: Recommendation system may reduce stakeholder autonomy or create perceived coercion
Potential Impact: Stakeholders may resist the system or experience unfair pressure to follow recommendations.
Mitigation Strategy: Keep recommendations advisory, explain rationale, allow opt-out, and monitor acceptance and fairness.
Residual Limitation: Power asymmetry between university, vendors, and students may still influence behavior.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Opt-out rate; stakeholder feedback; fairness audit

Threat: Data collection may raise privacy and consent concerns
Potential Impact: Student or vendor data may be misused or collected without adequate transparency.
Mitigation Strategy: Use anonymization, consent procedures, data minimization, access control, and ethical review.
Residual Limitation: Even anonymized behavioral data can sometimes be reidentified.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Data governance protocol; privacy impact assessment

Threat: Optimization may produce unfair allocation of attention, subsidies, or demand
Potential Impact: Certain vendors or student groups may systematically benefit less from MOEE recommendations.
Mitigation Strategy: Include fairness constraints, report distributional impacts, and review recommendations with stakeholders.
Residual Limitation: Fairness definitions may differ across stakeholders.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Fairness metrics; subgroup analysis; stakeholder review

Threat: Simulation environment may not capture informal negotiation, trust, culture, and institutional routines
Potential Impact: Digital twin behavior may diverge from real organizational dynamics.
Mitigation Strategy: Position the Mini-Lab as a decision-support and hypothesis-testing tool, not a perfect replica of reality.
Residual Limitation: Full social complexity cannot be simulated exhaustively.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Pilot study comparison; qualitative stakeholder feedback

Threat: Short simulation horizons may miss long-term adaptation and path dependence
Potential Impact: Long-run vendor exit, trust change, habit formation, or institutional learning may be underestimated.
Affected MOEE Component: Simulation horizon; PUDAL learning; stakeholder state
Mitigation Strategy: Run short-, medium-, and long-horizon experiments and report horizon sensitivity.
Residual Limitation: Long-term dynamics require additional behavioral and institutional assumptions.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: 30, 60, and 180 time-step comparison

Threat: MOEE terminology may be difficult for external readers to map to established systems-engineering concepts
Potential Impact: The contribution may be misunderstood or considered overly domain-specific.
Mitigation Strategy: Provide clear definitions, notation, comparison with literature streams, and self-contained tables.
Residual Limitation: New frameworks require repeated explanation and empirical demonstration.
Evidence / Diagnostic Check: Reviewer feedback; terminology glossary; comparison tables
